ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for 'tagliere di salumi' (Italian assorted cured meat platter). FOODON_03315116 (cured meat food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (cured/preserved meat products) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for a mixed Italian salumi platter.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for a mixed Italian cured meat platter presentation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical tagliere di salumi serving for one to two people consists of approximately 200 g of assorted sliced cured meats arranged on a wooden board, often accompanied by bread, olives, and pickles.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Italian-style salami, dry or hard; prosciutto; mortadella composite estimate for mixed Italian cured meat platter
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: A tagliere di salumi is a mixed platter of assorted Italian cured meats. No single USDA entry covers this composite dish. Values are estimated as a weighted average of representative Italian cured meats from USDA FoodData Central: Italian-style dry salami (FDC ID 174608), prosciutto (FDC ID 168322), and mortadella (FDC ID 174607), assuming approximately equal proportions. This is a reasonable approximation for a generic mixed salumi platter. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein- and fat-dominant foods — analogous estimate based on cured/processed meat category; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tagliere di salumi. Cured meats are high-protein, high-fat, very low-carbohydrate foods. The University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) classify protein- and fat-dominant meat products as having a GI of approximately 28 (low), consistent with the negligible glycemic carbohydrate content of cured meats. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (28 × 5.0) / 100 = 1.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~1350 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit or avoid tagliere di salumi, as a 200 g serving delivers approximately 2700 mg of sodium — exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and regular consumption of high-sodium cured meats is strongly associated with elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content with negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tagliere di salumi has a very low glycemic index (~28) and minimal carbohydrate content (~2.5 g per 100 g), making it unlikely to cause acute postprandial glucose excursions; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the high sodium and saturated fat content when consuming cured meats regularly.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat, high sodium, and high cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Frequent consumption of tagliere di salumi is not recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease, as the combination of high saturated fat (~8.5 g per 100 g), high sodium (~1350 mg per 100 g), and moderate cholesterol (~72 mg per 100 g) is associated with increased LDL cholesterol and elevated cardiovascular risk; occasional small portions may be acceptable within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tagliere di Salumi is a traditional Italian antipasto consisting of a curated platter of assorted cured and air-dried meats, typically served on a wooden board (tagliere). The word 'salumi' refers broadly to Italian cured meat products, encompassing a wide variety including prosciutto crudo (dry-cured ham), salame (fermented and dried sausage), mortadella (cooked pork sausage with fat inclusions), bresaola (air-dried salted beef), coppa (cured pork neck), and speck (smoked and cured ham from South Tyrol). The meats are sliced thin and arranged artfully, often accompanied by bread, grissini (breadsticks), olives, pickled vegetables, and sometimes cheese. In Italian culinary tradition, the tagliere di salumi is a quintessential antipasto or aperitivo course, meant for sharing. Nutritionally, cured meats are high in protein (~22 g per 100 g) and fat (~24 g per 100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (~2.5 g per 100 g), resulting in a low glycemic index (~28). However, they are very high in sodium (~1350 mg per 100 g), making them a concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The saturated fat content (~8.5 g per 100 g) is also clinically relevant for cardiovascular risk management. Despite the low glycemic impact, regular consumption of processed and cured meats has been associated with increased risk of colorectal cancer and cardiovascular disease in epidemiological studies. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian restaurants such as Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore and is typically priced as a premium sharing starter.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in Italian cured meats?
What meats are on an Italian charcuterie board?
Is tagliere di salumi high in fat?
What is the glycemic index of salumi?
Is prosciutto on a salumi board?

confidence_reason: No single approved source covers a composite tagliere di salumi platter. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted average of representative Italian cured meats from USDA FoodData Central (Italian-style dry salami, prosciutto, mortadella). GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) for protein- and fat-dominant meat products. Confidence is medium due to the composite and analogous estimation approach.
notes: 1. The input record incorrectly classified this food under 'Grains and staples'; it has been reclassified to 'Meat and alternatives' as tagliere di salumi is a cured meat platter. 2. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central for representative Italian cured meats (dry salami, prosciutto, mortadella) in approximately equal proportions. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as cured meats contain no dietary fibre. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as cured meats contain negligible vitamin C. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the composite source entries used. 6. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the composite source entries used. 7. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source entries used for this composite estimate. 8. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the composite source entries used. 9. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the composite source entries used. 10. GI is set to 28 (low) based on analogous estimate for protein- and fat-dominant cured meat products per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008). 11. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (28 × 5.0 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.4. 12. This is an Italian cuisine dish; multilingual naming rules for Italian cuisine do not mandate non-Latin script local names. No Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese mandatory language rules apply. 13. The food is served at Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore (Boat Quay, Singapore) per field survey source data.
